title: what i think about xenobots
body:
one of the things i keep coming back to is: what happens to people whose edge was logic, once AI outspeeds them at logic?

i've been in that category for a while. computers, abstractions, systems — that was my leverage. the gap is narrowing fast, and i don't think the interesting question is whether to panic. it's what to orient toward next.

the frame i've landed on is: go where the data is thin, where the atoms actually matter, where nobody has written the textbook yet. two fields keep bubbling up — biotech and space — and a specific corner of biotech has had my attention for a while: xenobots.

xenobots are small clusters of living cells that are not any animal. the genome is wild-type (originally xenopus laevis frog cells, more recently adult human tracheal cells), but the body plan is designed — by an evolutionary algorithm searching in silico over morphologies — and then either sculpted or allowed to self-assemble in a dish. they swim, they coordinate, they heal when cut, they carry cargo. a millimetric blob of skin cells that behaves like a robot.

the bit that broke my intuition was kinematic self-replication. parents shaped like a semi-toroidal pac-man swim through a dish of loose stem cells, bulldoze them into their concave mouth, and once the pile crosses a cohesion threshold a new ciliated sphere buds off and swims away. reproduction, but not genetic — mechanical. no animal on earth does this. the algorithm just found it by searching.

then came anthrobots. same idea, with adult human cells — tracheal epithelium from ordinary donors. no sculpting needed. drop them in matrigel and they self-assemble into 30–500 μm motile organisms, 100 to 1000 cells each. their cilia flip from inward-facing to outward-facing, and suddenly they swim. drop a cluster on a scratched monolayer of human neurons, and in about 72 hours the neural tissue bridges the gap. the bots contain zero neural cells. the mechanism is, in the authors' own words, unclear.

the interesting conceptual move under all this is that the genome stops looking like a blueprint. it looks more like a parts list. the shape is computed by the collective — voltage gradients across membranes, gap-junction coupling, what levin's group calls a bioelectric code. pattern V_mem, you pattern the body. planaria with their bioelectric target rewritten grow two heads and breed true, with the dna never touched.

if you squint, what you have is a substrate that is:
- cheap. the raw material is dna, and humans produce it in embarrassing quantities.
- programmable at the body-plan level, not the gene level.
- self-assembling, self-healing, self-replicating.
- mostly undocumented. a handful of labs, a few hundred papers, a lot of tacit wet-lab knowledge that nobody has put on the internet.

that last point is where the real moat is. a fine-tuned model can soak up everything humans have written — it can't soak up what we haven't written yet. and the hard part of this field — cell culture, morphogenetic feedback, bioelectric instrumentation — sits almost entirely in the unwritten part.

where i end up is something like: the most interesting work for the next decade might be the work that teaches the model, not the work the model can already do. biology at this layer feels like exactly that kind of work.

and once you have programmable living matter, the space angle gets strange. you don't need heavy infrastructure to seed a planet — you need cells, a target morphology, and a launch window. mechanical work done by millions of microscopic organisms that were evolved in silico. terraforming as a self-replicating process. it reads like science fiction until you remember that the pac-man replicators already exist, in a dish, today.

none of this is certain. it might take a century. it might hit a wall i can't see. but when i ask myself where the frontier still needs wet atoms and original thinking, this is where the arrow points.

title: reading before writing
body:
i'd like to write down my reflections honestly. but whenever i try, most of what comes out feels too basic to be worth reading — probably already thought through centuries ago and written down better, in books we wouldn't even call philosophical by today's standards.

if i actually want to think something that hasn't already been said better, i first need to catch up. at minimum, reach the level of the canonical philosophers — the ones already read by millions before me.

so that's where i'm starting: reading. trying to do it every day, juggling 2–3 books at once. marx and nietzsche first.

balancing this with my day job isn't easy. work eats most of my time. but i'm making room where i can. one book every two months feels like an honest pace — realistic without being lazy.

title: what am i thinking about
body: open thread. half-formed ideas, questions, things i can't stop chewing on.
